hud-fidelity.rationale: Sparkline test passes: the proposed-direction sketch shows '▁▂▂▃▅▇▇▆  7/min' — eight cells, fixed alphabet, inside an existing line of the band. Spellcheck test passes: the gutter '●' and end-of-row '✗' both ride the line of work itself, not a popup. But two data-ink offences keep this off 9. (1) Double-encoding of 'freshest': the sketch keeps Tier-1 bold on the description AND a gutter '●' in column 0 — same signal carried twice, which Tufte's 'is this ink earning its keep?' rejects. The gutter is the novel HUD primitive; the bold is reflex. (2) The sparkline label '7/min' restates what the shape already shows; unless 60s-rate precision is load-bearing, the digits are redundant ink. (3) Per-row primitive count is borderline four — gutter cell + column-1 verb glyph + dim/bold weight run + end-of-row badge — at the ceiling the brief sets. (4) Most importantly, the verb-glyph column is the design's small-multiples instrument, but Q4 explicitly leaves its dictionary, fallback, and suppression rules unresolved ('Some agent activities … don't have natural glyphs. Fallback options: …'). If column 1 sometimes carries a glyph and sometimes doesn't, the eye cannot read verb-shape directly — it becomes decorative chrome and breaks the brief's small-multiples-discipline rule. The eyebrow source row (Tier 4, blank above and below in the sketch) also spends a whole row on a 7-char string — low data-ink ratio versus the existing inline-prefix.

four-questions.rationale: All four are answerable by glance in the proposed direction: liveness from the sparkline's existence, pattern from its shape, exception from the row-end '✗' (plus tone), latest from the column-0 '●'. The information shape is mostly perceived directly (sparkline → rate). Two weaknesses keep this off 9. Exception is encoded as a single end-of-row glyph rather than as gutter mass: a column of '✗' marks accumulating down the band would let the eye count exceptions without reading them — the canonical Tufte 'gutter mass → exception count' move is left on the table while the design instead tucks the badge into the row-end where truncation collides with it (Q8 still open). Pattern via sparkline conflates 'stuck' and 'idle' — both render as flat '▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁'; the candidate flags this implicitly in Q7 (empty state) but doesn't distinguish a stalled agent (recent-but-unchanging) from an empty band. Latest is over-encoded (gutter + bold) rather than carried by one disciplined primitive.

terminal-feasibility.rationale: All glyphs are attainable: '▁▂▃▄▅▆▇█' is unconditional unicode; '●' '✓' '✗' are basic; the verb glyphs Q9 lists ('nf-md-eye'/'book-open', 'format-list-bulleted', 'magnify', 'pencil', 'play-circle') are nerd-font codepoints inside the brief's ≤6 budget. 30-col width is comfortable: the sparkline header is 8 + 1 + 6 = 15 cols, and the row pattern '● _ description … ✗' fits with margin. The lens-relevant feasibility loss is that the four states are unevenly mocked: empty is enumerated as a question in Q7 (sparkline-suppressed? flat? phrase only?), multi-source is acknowledged in Q5 as 'eyebrows expensive' with no mock, error-heavy has no sketch despite being the state where gutter-mass would most pay off, and narrow-30 has no separate mock either. With ten open questions still on the page, the design is implementable but the small-multiples discipline that the implementor would be asked to enforce row-by-row is not yet specified.

asi_candidate.summary: Close the verb-glyph column's small-multiples discipline: make column 1 a guaranteed single-glyph slot on every data row.
asi_candidate.actionable_change: In §Proposed direction, replace Q4 (verb glyph dictionary size) with a resolved spec: column 1 of every data row carries exactly one glyph from a closed three-class dictionary — (a) tool-verb glyphs (read/list/search/edit/run, with the five vocab.ts codepoints from Q9 promoted out of the open list and into the spec proper), (b) severity glyphs from the existing vocab.ts severity set when the row is a status transition (working/waiting/ready/stopped/error), (c) a single explicit generic fallback ('nf-md-chevron-right' or '▶') for any tool call outside the closed verb set. Forbid empty column-1 cells and forbid stacking verb+severity. State the rule explicitly: 'one glyph, one cell, every row — so the column reads as verb-shape over time.' Then add a one-line example showing a stretch of rows where the column reads as a small-multiples shape (e.g. '          ▶  ' = four reads, one search, one fallback). This is the minimum a Tufte read demands before the verb column earns its ink.
asi_candidate.why_this_lens: The candidate's strongest HUD-primitive bet is the column-1 instrument, but the spec leaves it conditionally applied. The brief's lens explicitly penalises 'designs where the same instrument is unevenly applied across rows (small-multiples discipline broken)' and rewards 'designs where the information shape is perceived directly … rather than inferred from text.' Until column 1 is guaranteed-populated and closed-dictionary, the eye cannot read it as shape; resolving this in iteration-1 converts the column from decorative chrome into the second perceptible-by-glance instrument after the sparkline.
asi_candidate.scope: single-section edit to docs/simmer/activity-zone/iteration-1-candidate.md: rewrite Q4 + Q9 into a resolved §Verb-glyph column subsection inside §Proposed direction; remove those items from §Open design questions.

notes_for_phase_2: If another lens proposes dropping the verb-glyph column entirely, my ASI dies cleanly — the column either commits to small-multiples discipline or it goes; either resolves the broken-instrument penalty.

Secondary Tufte concern not in the ASI: the freshest-row signal is double-encoded (gutter '●' AND Tier-1 bold). Worth flagging in synthesis if no other panelist raises it — single-encoding would let all descriptions ride at one weight.
Tertiary: the sparkline's '7/min' suffix is redundant with the shape; a max-tick or unitless could replace it. Lower priority than the column discipline.
